enrichment.short_description: Nhost is an open-source backend-as-a-service platform that bundles a managed Postgres database, an instant GraphQL API powered by Hasura, authentication, file storage, and serverless functions into a single developer platform.
enrichment.seo_title: Nhost Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nhost review 2026: see how this open-source Postgres and GraphQL backend platform compares on features, pricing tiers, and top Firebase and Supabase alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nhost is an open-source backend-as-a-service (BaaS) platform founded in 2019 and headquartered in Stockholm, Sweden. It positions itself as an open-source alternative to Google Firebase, built around PostgreSQL rather than a proprietary NoSQL store. The core product bundles five components into one managed platform: a dedicated PostgreSQL database per project, an automatically generated GraphQL API powered by the open-source Hasura GraphQL Engine (with realtime subscriptions and granular row-level permissions), an authentication service supporting email/password, magic links, and OAuth providers such as Google and GitHub, an S3-compatible file storage service, and serverless Functions written in Node.js/TypeScript that run alongside the API. Nhost also ships a CLI and Infrastructure-as-Code tooling so developers can run a full local development environment and deploy changes through git-based workflows. In April 2021 Nhost raised a 3 million dollar seed round led by Nauta Capital, with participation from Antler and angel investors including the founders of GitHub and Netlify, underscoring its positioning as developer-first infrastructure. The company is a small, distributed team; public revenue trackers have reported roughly 660,000 dollars in annual revenue on a team of around six people in 2025, indicating a lean, profitable-leaning operation rather than a heavily headcount-scaled one. Pricing runs on a freemium model: a free Starter tier offers 1 GB database, 1 GB storage, and 5 GB egress on a single project that pauses after a week of inactivity; the Pro tier starts at 25 dollars per month (including 15 dollars of compute credits) and adds automated backups, point-in-time recovery, and managed Grafana observability; a Team tier starting at 599 dollars per month adds SOC 2 Type II compliance, SLA-backed support, and external database connectivity; and Enterprise is custom-priced with dedicated clusters and a technical account manager. Nhost targets indie developers, startups, and small-to-medium engineering teams who want a Postgres-native backend without operating their own infrastructure, competing directly with Supabase (its closest analog, also Postgres-based), Firebase, Hasura Cloud, AWS Amplify, and Appwrite. Its main differentiators versus Supabase are its native, tight integration with Hasura's GraphQL engine (versus Supabase's REST/PostgREST-first approach) and its emphasis on GraphQL as the primary API layer. Because the entire stack -- database schema, Hasura metadata, auth configuration, and functions -- is open source and self-hostable, Nhost also appeals to teams wanting to avoid vendor lock-in while still using a managed cloud offering for production workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nhost is an open-source backend platform built to give developers a production-ready backend without writing custom server code. At its core is a managed PostgreSQL database paired with the Hasura GraphQL Engine, which automatically exposes the database schema as a fully-featured GraphQL API complete with realtime subscriptions and fine-grained permissions.

Founded in 2019 and based in Stockholm, Sweden, Nhost is frequently described as an open-source alternative to Firebase, but with a relational Postgres foundation instead of a proprietary NoSQL database. The platform bundles authentication, file storage, and serverless functions alongside the database and API, so teams can build a complete application backend from a single dashboard and CLI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The GraphQL API is generated directly from the Postgres schema, meaning developers get queries, mutations, and live subscriptions as soon as tables are created, without hand-writing resolvers. Row-level permissions can be attached per role, and remote schemas let teams stitch in third-party APIs or legacy services alongside their own data.

Authentication supports email and password, passwordless magic links, and OAuth sign-in with providers like Google and GitHub, with unlimited users included even on the free tier. Storage is S3-compatible for files and media, and serverless Functions written in Node.js or TypeScript can run custom business logic that hooks into the same GraphQL layer, all deployable through the Nhost CLI and git-based Infrastructure-as-Code workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nhost uses a usage-based freemium model. The free Starter plan is meant for prototyping and side projects, capped at 1 GB of database storage and 1 GB of file storage on a single project that pauses after a week without traffic.

Paid plans begin with Pro at 25 dollars per month, which raises resource limits and adds automated backups and monitoring, followed by Team at 599 dollars per month for SOC 2 compliance and SLA support, and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier for dedicated infrastructure and account management.

features.title: Managed Postgres database
features.description: Each project gets a dedicated PostgreSQL instance rather than a shared or proprietary datastore, giving developers full SQL access and standard relational tooling.

features.title: Instant GraphQL API via Hasura
features.description: The open-source Hasura GraphQL Engine auto-generates queries, mutations, and realtime subscriptions directly from the database schema, with granular role-based permissions.

features.title: Built-in authentication
features.description: Supports email and password, passwordless magic links, and OAuth providers such as Google and GitHub, with unlimited authenticated users on every plan.

features.title: S3-compatible file storage
features.description: Upload, transform, and serve files and media through a storage service that integrates directly with the auth and permissions layer.

features.title: Serverless Functions
features.description: Custom backend logic can be written in Node.js or TypeScript and deployed as serverless functions that run alongside the GraphQL API.

features.title: CLI and Infrastructure as Code
features.description: A local development CLI mirrors the cloud environment, and configuration can be version-controlled and deployed through git-based workflows.

features.title: Realtime subscriptions
features.description: GraphQL subscriptions push live data updates to connected clients automatically whenever underlying rows change, without manual WebSocket setup.

features.title: Remote schema stitching
features.description: External REST APIs and other GraphQL services can be merged into a single unified Nhost GraphQL endpoint.

pros: Fully open source and self-hostable, reducing vendor lock-in risk
Built on standard PostgreSQL rather than a proprietary database format
GraphQL API is generated automatically, saving significant backend development time
Generous free tier suitable for prototypes and small projects
Backed by well-known angel investors from GitHub and Netlify, signaling strong developer-tooling pedigree
cons: Smaller team and ecosystem than larger competitors like Supabase or Firebase, which may mean slower feature velocity
GraphQL-first approach requires familiarity with GraphQL and Hasura concepts, which adds a learning curve for teams used to REST
Jump from Pro to Team pricing (25 dollars to 599 dollars per month) is steep for growing teams
Free tier projects pause after a week of inactivity, which can be disruptive for infrequently used demos
Smaller community and fewer third-party integrations compared to more established BaaS platforms

faqs.question: What is Nhost used for?
faqs.answer: Nhost is used as a backend-as-a-service for web and mobile apps, providing a managed Postgres database, an auto-generated GraphQL API, authentication, file storage, and serverless functions so developers do not need to build and host their own backend infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is Nhost open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nhost's core stack, including its database tooling, Hasura-based GraphQL layer, authentication service, and storage service, is open source and can be self-hosted, in addition to being available as a managed cloud service.

faqs.question: How is Nhost different from Supabase?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, Postgres-based backend platforms, but Nhost centers its API layer on GraphQL through Hasura, while Supabase centers its API layer on REST through PostgREST. Teams that prefer GraphQL as their primary API paradigm tend to gravitate toward Nhost.

faqs.question: Does Nhost have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nhost offers a free Starter plan with 1 GB of database storage, 1 GB of file storage, and 5 GB of egress, suitable for small projects and prototypes, though the single free project pauses after a week of inactivity.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Nhost support for functions?
faqs.answer: Nhost serverless Functions are written in Node.js using JavaScript or TypeScript.

faqs.question: Who is behind Nhost?
faqs.answer: Nhost is a Stockholm, Sweden-based startup founded in 2019 that raised a 3 million dollar seed round in 2021 led by Nauta Capital, with angel backing from the founders of GitHub and Netlify.

faqs.question: Does Nhost support realtime data?
faqs.answer: Yes, through GraphQL subscriptions, Nhost pushes live updates to connected clients automatically whenever the underlying database rows change, without requiring custom WebSocket infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Startup MVP backend
use_cases.description: Early-stage startups use Nhost to stand up a full backend, database, auth, and API in days instead of weeks, letting small engineering teams focus on the frontend and product experience.
use_cases.audience: Startup founders and small engineering teams

use_cases.title: GraphQL-first applications
use_cases.description: Teams already committed to a GraphQL API layer use Nhost to get an instant, permission-aware GraphQL endpoint generated directly from their Postgres schema, avoiding hand-written resolvers.
use_cases.audience: Frontend and full-stack developers building GraphQL clients

use_cases.title: Self-hosted backend for regulated environments
use_cases.description: Because the stack is open source, organizations with data residency or compliance requirements can self-host Nhost's components on their own infrastructure instead of using the managed cloud.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises and regulated industries with strict data control needs

features: 10 GB database, 50 GB storage, 50 GB egress

enrichment.short_description: NiceJob is a reputation marketing platform that automates review requests, referrals, and social proof sharing to help local service businesses win more customers.
enrichment.seo_title: NiceJob Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: NiceJob review 2026: explore this reputation marketing platform's review automation features, pricing plans, pros and cons, and top competing alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: NiceJob is a reputation marketing and review management platform founded in 2017 and headquartered in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, built to help local service businesses such as contractors, home service companies, salons, and franchises generate and showcase customer reviews. The platform automates the process of requesting reviews after a job or purchase, monitors reviews across platforms like Google and Facebook, and surfaces positive feedback through social proof widgets embedded on a business's website and a shareable microsite. In October 2021, NiceJob was acquired by Paystone, a Canadian payments and customer engagement company, and now operates as part of the Paystone family of products alongside payment processing and loyalty tools, while retaining its own brand and standalone product. NiceJob's core plans are the Reviews plan at 75 dollars per month, which covers automated review requests and follow-up reminders, multi-platform review monitoring, social proof widgets, AI-assisted review responses, staff leaderboards, and a branded microsite; and the Pro plan at 125 dollars per month, which adds automated booking reminders, referral campaigns, automated gifting for repeat customers, competitor SEO insight tracking, and NPS survey collection for deeper customer feedback. NiceJob also offers website-focused bundles: a Sites Only plan at 99 dollars per month (plus a 199 dollar setup fee) that includes a custom-built, SEO-optimized website with a guaranteed sales-lift promise, and a combined Grow plus Sites bundle at 174 dollars per month. All plans come with a 14-day free trial, no long-term contracts, and custom pricing for multi-location franchises. The company reports team sizes in the range of roughly 30 to 40 employees according to third-party data providers. NiceJob's target audience is small and mid-sized local service businesses -- particularly home services, contractors, salons, and franchises -- that depend heavily on online reviews and word-of-mouth referrals to win new customers. Its primary competitors in the reputation management and local marketing space include Birdeye, Podium, Grade.us, Broadly, and ReviewTrackers, with NiceJob differentiating itself through simplicity, an emphasis on 'nice' automated customer touchpoints (thank-you notes, gifting, referrals) rather than purely transactional review requests, and tight bundling with Paystone's payments ecosystem for businesses that already process payments through Paystone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NiceJob is a reputation marketing platform built specifically for local service businesses that rely on online reviews and referrals to win new customers. Founded in 2017 in Vancouver, Canada, the platform automates the unglamorous parts of reputation management: asking happy customers for reviews at the right moment, monitoring what gets posted across review sites, and turning that positive feedback into marketing material.

In 2021, NiceJob was acquired by Paystone, a Canadian payments and customer engagement company, and now operates as part of Paystone's broader suite of small-business tools while continuing to run under its own brand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's core loop is automated review requests sent via text or email after a completed job, paired with monitoring across major review platforms so businesses can respond quickly to feedback. Positive reviews are automatically pulled into social proof widgets for the business's website and a dedicated microsite that can be shared on social media.

Higher-tier plans extend beyond reviews into growth marketing: automated referral campaigns that reward customers for sending new business, automated gifting to reward loyal customers, booking reminder automation to bring customers back, and Net Promoter Score surveys to capture deeper feedback than a star rating alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: NiceJob's core software pricing starts at 75 dollars per month for the Reviews plan and steps up to 125 dollars per month for the Pro plan, which adds referrals, gifting, and NPS surveys.

For businesses that also need a website, NiceJob offers bundled plans starting at 99 dollars per month plus a one-time 199 dollar setup fee, with a combined website-and-marketing bundle at 174 dollars per month. All plans include a 14-day free trial and no long-term contract.

features.title: Automated review requests
features.description: Sends timed text and email requests asking recent customers to leave a review, with automated follow-up reminders to increase response rates.

features.title: Multi-platform review monitoring
features.description: Tracks new reviews across Google, Facebook, and other major platforms from a single dashboard so businesses never miss feedback.

features.title: Social proof widgets
features.description: Automatically displays recent positive reviews on the business's website to build trust with prospective customers.

features.title: Branded microsite
features.description: Generates a shareable microsite that showcases a business's best reviews, useful for social media and marketing campaigns.

features.title: Referral automation
features.description: Encourages happy customers to refer new business through automated, trackable referral campaigns (available on Pro plan).

features.title: AI-assisted review responses
features.description: Uses AI to help draft timely responses to customer reviews, reducing the manual effort of reputation management.

features.title: NPS surveys
features.description: Collects Net Promoter Score feedback to identify at-risk customers and advocates beyond simple star ratings (Pro plan).

features.title: Staff leaderboards
features.description: Gamifies review generation by ranking staff members on review volume and ratings to encourage friendly competition.

pros: Purpose-built for local service businesses with straightforward setup
Combines review generation with referral and gifting automation, not just review requests
Includes social proof widgets and a shareable microsite out of the box
14-day free trial with no long-term contract required
Backed by Paystone's broader payments and customer engagement ecosystem
cons: Pricing is higher than some basic review-request-only tools
Website bundle plans carry an additional one-time setup fee
Advanced features like referrals and NPS surveys are locked behind the higher Pro tier
Primarily focused on English-language markets and North American review platforms
Multi-location and franchise pricing requires a custom quote rather than transparent published rates

faqs.question: What does NiceJob do?
faqs.answer: NiceJob automates review requests, review monitoring, referral campaigns, and social proof marketing to help local service businesses build their online reputation and win more customers.

faqs.question: How much does NiceJob cost?
faqs.answer: NiceJob's Reviews plan starts at 75 dollars per month, with a Pro plan at 125 dollars per month that adds referrals, gifting, and NPS surveys. Website bundle plans start at 99 dollars per month plus a 199 dollar setup fee.

faqs.question: Is NiceJob owned by Paystone?
faqs.answer: Yes, NiceJob was acquired by Paystone, a Canadian payments and customer engagement company, in October 2021, and continues to operate under its own brand as part of Paystone's product suite.

faqs.question: Does NiceJob offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, NiceJob offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required and no long-term contract.

faqs.question: What industries use NiceJob?
faqs.answer: NiceJob is designed primarily for local service businesses such as contractors, home service companies, salons, and franchises that depend on reviews and referrals for growth.

faqs.question: Does NiceJob build websites?
faqs.answer: Yes, through its Sites Only and Grow plus Sites bundles, NiceJob offers custom-designed, SEO-optimized websites with a personal website coach and call tracking, in addition to its core reputation marketing tools.

faqs.question: Who are NiceJob's main competitors?
faqs.answer: NiceJob competes with reputation management platforms such as Birdeye, Podium, Broadly, Grade.us, and ReviewTrackers.

use_cases.title: Automating review generation for contractors
use_cases.description: Home service contractors use NiceJob to automatically request reviews after each completed job, building a steady stream of fresh testimonials without manual follow-up.

use_cases.title: Turning happy customers into referrals
use_cases.description: Service businesses use NiceJob's referral and gifting automation to convert satisfied customers into new leads, reducing reliance on paid advertising.
use_cases.audience: Local service businesses focused on word-of-mouth growth

use_cases.title: Multi-location franchise reputation management
use_cases.description: Franchise brands use NiceJob's custom multi-location pricing to standardize review monitoring and social proof marketing across all locations from a central dashboard.

enrichment.short_description: Nightwatch (nightwatch.io) is an SEO rank tracking and search visibility platform that monitors keyword rankings across Google, Bing, YouTube, and AI platforms like ChatGPT and Gemini, with hyperlocal grid tracking and white-label reporting.
enrichment.seo_title: Nightwatch Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nightwatch review 2026: see how this SEO rank tracking and AI visibility platform compares on features, pricing plans, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nightwatch, at nightwatch.io, is an SEO rank tracking and search intelligence platform founded in 2016 by Aljaz Fajmut, Matic Jurglic, and Steve Mingione, and headquartered in Ljubljana, Slovenia. Note that this Nightwatch is a distinct product and company from Nightwatch.js, the unrelated open-source browser test automation framework -- nightwatch.io is purpose-built for SEO monitoring, not software testing. The platform grew from a specialized rank tracker known for hyperlocal precision, tracking rankings down to the zip-code level across more than 107,000 locations in over 190 countries, into a broader search intelligence suite. It tracks rankings across Google, Bing, Yahoo, DuckDuckGo, and YouTube, and as of 2026 has expanded into AI visibility monitoring, tracking how a brand is cited and recommended inside AI answer engines such as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity, with a 'Citation Intelligence' feature that connects traditional Google SERP rankings to AI-driven recommendations and traces AI mentions through to resulting site traffic. Other core features include automated technical site audits with Core Web Vitals monitoring and smart issue prioritization, SERP feature tracking (featured snippets, knowledge panels, local packs), keyword segmentation by intent or custom tags, white-label PDF and dashboard reporting, a Looker Studio integration, API access, and an automation feature called the NightOwl Agent that performs competitor gap analysis and content optimization suggestions. Nightwatch prices on a per-keyword, unlimited-seat model rather than per-user licensing: Starter costs 79 euros per month (500 keywords, 5 websites, 50 AI prompts monthly), Professional costs 159 euros per month (2,500 keywords, 25 websites, 150 AI prompts, API access), Agency costs 399 euros per month (7,500 keywords, 100 websites, 500 AI prompts, dedicated account manager, SSO), and Enterprise is custom-priced for 20,000-plus keywords with dedicated infrastructure. Annual billing saves roughly 20 percent versus monthly. All plans include unlimited team seats, a 14-day free trial, SOC 2 compliance, and three years of historical SERP data. The company reports serving more than 10,000 teams globally with roughly 11 to 50 employees. Nightwatch's target audience spans SEO agencies, in-house marketing teams, and enterprises that need granular local rank tracking and, increasingly, visibility into how their brand appears in AI-generated search answers. Its primary competitors include Semrush, Ahrefs, Moz Pro, AccuRanker, SE Ranking, and other rank-tracking and AI-visibility tools, with Nightwatch differentiating itself through unlimited seats with no per-user fees, extreme local tracking granularity, and early investment in AI-platform citation tracking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nightwatch is an SEO rank tracking and search intelligence platform available at nightwatch.io, founded in 2016 in Ljubljana, Slovenia. It should not be confused with Nightwatch.js, an unrelated open-source browser automation testing framework -- this Nightwatch is a marketing and SEO product built for tracking search visibility.

The platform started as a highly granular local rank tracker, capable of monitoring keyword positions down to the zip-code level across more than 107,000 locations worldwide, and has since expanded into a full search intelligence suite covering traditional search engines, YouTube, and, more recently, AI answer engines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core rank tracking covers Google, Bing, Yahoo, DuckDuckGo, and YouTube with claimed 99.9 percent accuracy backed by raw HTML snapshots for verification, plus real-time refresh for priority keywords. Site audit tools flag technical SEO issues and Core Web Vitals problems with smart prioritization so teams know what to fix first.

Nightwatch's newer AI Visibility features monitor how a brand is mentioned and recommended inside ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity, connecting those AI citations back to traditional Google rankings and downstream site traffic. Reporting is white-labeled by default across every plan, with automated daily reports, SERP feature tracking, and a Looker Studio integration for custom dashboards.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nightwatch prices per keyword volume rather than per seat, with unlimited team members included on every plan. The Starter plan costs 79 euros per month for 500 keywords and 5 websites, Professional costs 159 euros per month for 2,500 keywords and API access, and Agency costs 399 euros per month for 7,500 keywords with a dedicated account manager and SSO.

Enterprise pricing is custom for organizations tracking more than 20,000 keywords. Annual billing saves approximately 20 percent compared to paying monthly, and every plan includes a 14-day full-feature free trial.

features.title: Multi-engine rank tracking
features.description: Tracks keyword positions across Google, Bing, Yahoo, DuckDuckGo, and YouTube with daily updates and raw HTML snapshot verification.

features.title: Hyperlocal grid tracking
features.description: Monitors local search rankings down to the zip-code level across more than 107,000 locations in over 190 countries, ideal for multi-location and local SEO.

features.title: AI visibility and citation tracking
features.description: Monitors brand mentions and recommendations inside AI platforms like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity, and connects those citations to traditional search rankings and traffic.

features.title: Technical site audits
features.description: Runs automated site audits that flag technical SEO issues and Core Web Vitals problems with smart prioritization of fixes.

features.title: White-label reporting
features.description: Every plan includes white-label PDF and dashboard reporting, useful for agencies reporting to clients under their own brand.

features.title: SERP feature tracking
features.description: Tracks featured snippets, knowledge panels, local packs, and other SERP features alongside standard organic rankings.

features.title: NightOwl Agent automation
features.description: An AI-driven agent that performs competitor gap analysis and content optimization suggestions.

features.title: Unlimited team seats
features.description: All pricing tiers include unlimited user seats with no per-seat fees, unlike many competing rank trackers.

pros: Unlimited team seats on every plan with no per-user fees
Very granular local rank tracking, down to the zip-code level
Early and dedicated investment in AI-platform visibility tracking
White-label reporting included on all tiers, useful for agencies
14-day free trial with full feature access
cons: Priced in euros, which can add currency conversion friction for some customers
Keyword caps on lower tiers can be limiting for larger sites or agencies
Smaller feature footprint outside core SEO than all-in-one suites like Semrush or Ahrefs
AI prompt monitoring quotas are capped per plan, requiring upgrades for heavy AI-visibility tracking
Enterprise-grade features like SSO and dedicated account management require the top Agency tier or above

faqs.question: What is Nightwatch (nightwatch.io)?
faqs.answer: Nightwatch is an SEO rank tracking and search intelligence platform that monitors keyword rankings across search engines and, more recently, AI answer engines, with a focus on hyperlocal tracking and white-label reporting.

faqs.question: Is Nightwatch the same as Nightwatch.js?
faqs.answer: No. Nightwatch.io is an SEO and search visibility product, while Nightwatch.js is a separate, unrelated open-source JavaScript end-to-end testing framework for browsers.

faqs.question: How much does Nightwatch cost?
faqs.answer: Nightwatch's Starter plan starts at 79 euros per month for 500 keywords, with Professional at 159 euros per month and Agency at 399 euros per month; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Does Nightwatch track AI search visibility?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nightwatch monitors how brands are cited and recommended inside AI platforms including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity, and links those mentions back to traditional SEO rankings.

faqs.question: Does Nightwatch charge per user?
faqs.answer: No, Nightwatch includes unlimited team seats on every plan, pricing instead by keyword and website volume.

faqs.question: Who founded Nightwatch?
faqs.answer: Nightwatch was founded in 2016 in Ljubljana, Slovenia by Aljaz Fajmut, Matic Jurglic, and Steve Mingione.

faqs.question: Does Nightwatch offer local rank tracking?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nightwatch offers highly granular local rank tracking down to the zip-code level across more than 107,000 locations worldwide.

use_cases.title: Agency client reporting
use_cases.description: SEO agencies use Nightwatch's white-label reporting and unlimited seats to manage rank tracking for multiple clients without paying per-user fees.

use_cases.title: Multi-location local SEO tracking
use_cases.description: Multi-location businesses and franchises use Nightwatch's hyperlocal grid tracking to monitor rankings at the zip-code level across every store location.

use_cases.title: AI search visibility monitoring
use_cases.description: Brands use Nightwatch's AI visibility tools to see how they are cited and recommended inside ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity as AI-driven search grows.
use_cases.audience: In-house SEO and content marketing teams

enrichment.short_description: Nimble is a relationship-focused CRM for small businesses that unifies contacts, social signals, email, and pipeline management, with deep integration into Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace.
enrichment.seo_title: Nimble Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nimble CRM review 2026: explore this small-business relationship management platform's features, pricing, integrations, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nimble is a relationship-focused CRM founded in 2009 and headquartered in Santa Monica, California. Its founder and CEO, Jon Ferrara, previously co-founded GoldMine, one of the earliest contact-management CRM products, in 1989, and sold it for a reported 125 million dollars before starting Nimble to bring a similar founder-led, relationship-first philosophy to a modern, socially-connected CRM. Nimble differentiates itself from larger CRMs like Salesforce and HubSpot by emphasizing simplicity and deep integration with the tools small businesses already use daily, particularly Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace, through a browser extension called Nimble Prospector that lets users capture contact and company data from any web page, social profile, or inbox. The platform combines contact management, deal and pipeline tracking, email marketing and sequencing, and social signal enrichment (pulling in social media activity and company data to build a fuller picture of each contact) into one system, with AI-powered features for drafting emails and summarizing relationship history. Nimble's core Business plan is priced at 24.90 dollars per seat per month billed annually (or 29.90 dollars per seat billed monthly), and includes 25,000 contact records, 2 GB of storage per seat, Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace integration, the Nimble Prospector browser extension, and 1,000 group messages per license per month, with a 14-day free trial requiring no credit card. Beyond the base plan, Nimble offers a la carte add-ons rather than multiple rigid tiers: email marketing (15 dollars per month company-wide, unlimited sends), web forms and live chat (12 dollars per month per team), data enrichment credits (10 dollars per month for 100 credits), additional contact capacity (10 dollars per month per additional 10,000 contacts), extra storage (10 dollars per month per additional 10 GB), and larger group-messaging packages (15 to 99 dollars per month for 2,000 to 25,000 emails). This modular pricing lets small businesses pay only for the capacity and features they need as they grow. Nimble's target audience is small businesses and solopreneurs, particularly those who already run their business communication through Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace and want a CRM that layers on top of existing email and calendar workflows rather than replacing them. The company is headquartered in Santa Monica with an employee count generally estimated in the low hundreds. Its primary competitors include HubSpot CRM, Zoho CRM, Insightly, Pipedrive, and Salesforce, with Nimble's key differentiators being its social-signal-driven contact enrichment, tight Office 365/Google Workspace integration, and a founder-led company culture inherited from Jon Ferrara's earlier success building GoldMine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nimble is a CRM built for small businesses that want a simpler alternative to enterprise platforms like Salesforce while still getting rich contact intelligence. Founded in 2009 in Santa Monica, California by Jon Ferrara -- who earlier co-founded and sold the pioneering CRM GoldMine -- Nimble applies a similar founder-led, relationship-first philosophy to a modern, cloud-based product.

The platform's defining idea is unifying contact management with the communication tools small businesses already use. Rather than asking teams to abandon their inbox and calendar, Nimble integrates deeply with Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace and layers social and web-sourced data on top of every contact record.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Nimble Prospector browser extension lets users capture contact, company, and social profile data from any webpage or inbox with a single click, automatically enriching CRM records. AI-powered tools help draft emails and summarize relationship history, while pipeline and deal tracking give sales teams visibility into where opportunities stand.

Group messaging and email marketing tools let small teams run outreach campaigns directly from the CRM, and add-on modules for web forms, live chat, and data enrichment let businesses expand functionality as they grow rather than being forced into a higher pricing tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nimble uses a single core Business plan at 24.90 dollars per seat per month when billed annually (29.90 dollars billed monthly), which includes 25,000 contact records and 2 GB of storage per seat.

Rather than multiple feature-gated tiers, Nimble sells modular add-ons for email marketing, web forms and chat, data enrichment, and extra contacts or storage, letting businesses pay only for what they use beyond the base plan. A 14-day free trial is available with no credit card required.

features.title: Nimble Prospector browser extension
features.description: Captures contact, company, and social data from any web page or inbox with one click, automatically enriching CRM records.

features.title: Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace integration
features.description: Syncs contacts, email, and calendar data directly with the tools small businesses already use daily.

features.title: AI-powered email creation
features.description: Uses AI to help draft outreach emails and summarize relationship history within a contact record.

features.title: Social signal enrichment
features.description: Pulls in social media activity and public data to build a fuller picture of each contact automatically.

features.title: Pipeline and deal management
features.description: Tracks deals through customizable sales pipelines with visibility into stage, value, and next steps.

features.title: Group messaging and email marketing
features.description: Sends segmented email campaigns directly from the CRM, with tiered monthly sending limits available as add-ons.

features.title: Contact and company records
features.description: Unifies contact history, notes, deals, and communications into a single relationship record accessible across the team.

features.title: Web forms and live chat add-on
features.description: Captures leads directly from a company website through embeddable forms and chat, feeding new contacts into the CRM.

pros: Deep, native integration with Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace
Founder-led company with a long track record in the CRM space via GoldMine
Social signal enrichment gives richer context on contacts than basic CRMs
Modular add-on pricing lets small businesses avoid paying for unused features
14-day free trial with no credit card required
cons: Single core plan with add-ons can make total cost less predictable than fixed tiers
Base plan's 25,000 contact and 2 GB storage limits require paid add-ons for larger businesses
Fewer advanced automation and reporting features than larger CRMs like HubSpot or Salesforce
Group messaging volume is capped and requires paid upgrades for larger campaigns
Best suited to small businesses; may lack depth for complex enterprise sales processes

faqs.question: What is Nimble CRM used for?
faqs.answer: Nimble is used by small businesses to manage contacts, track sales pipelines, and enrich customer records with social and web data, all integrated with Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace email and calendars.

faqs.question: How much does Nimble cost?
faqs.answer: Nimble's Business plan costs 24.90 dollars per seat per month billed annually, or 29.90 dollars per seat billed monthly, with additional add-ons available for email marketing, web forms, data enrichment, and extra storage or contacts.

faqs.question: Who founded Nimble?
faqs.answer: Nimble was founded in 2009 by Jon Ferrara, who previously co-founded the early CRM pioneer GoldMine in 1989 and sold it for a reported 125 million dollars.

faqs.question: Does Nimble integrate with Outlook and Gmail?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nimble integrates natively with both Microsoft 365 (including Outlook) and Google Workspace (including Gmail), syncing contacts, email, and calendar data.

faqs.question: Does Nimble offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nimble offers a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: What is Nimble Prospector?
faqs.answer: Nimble Prospector is a browser extension that lets users capture contact, company, and social profile information from any webpage or inbox directly into the CRM with one click.

faqs.question: How many contacts does Nimble support?
faqs.answer: The base Business plan includes 25,000 contact records per account, with additional blocks of 10,000 contacts available as a paid add-on.

use_cases.title: Solopreneur relationship management
use_cases.description: Independent consultants and solopreneurs use Nimble to keep track of every contact, follow-up, and deal without needing a dedicated sales operations team.

use_cases.title: Small sales team pipeline tracking
use_cases.description: Small sales teams use Nimble's pipeline and deal tracking, combined with Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace sync, to manage their sales process inside tools they already use.

use_cases.title: Social-driven lead capture
use_cases.description: Marketing and business development teams use Nimble Prospector to capture and enrich leads directly from social media and web browsing.
use_cases.audience: Small business marketing and BD teams

enrichment.short_description: NinjaOne is a unified IT and endpoint management platform offering remote monitoring and management (RMM), patch management, backup, and endpoint security for MSPs and internal IT teams.
enrichment.seo_title: NinjaOne Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: NinjaOne review 2026: see how this unified endpoint management and RMM platform compares on features, pricing, integrations, and top MSP tool alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: NinjaOne, originally launched as NinjaRMM, is a unified IT and endpoint management platform co-founded in 2013 by Sal Sferlazza and Chris Matarese, with its platform publicly released in 2015. The company is headquartered in Austin, Texas, with additional offices including Tampa, Berlin, Sydney, Melbourne, Singapore, Indonesia, and Seattle (the latter gained through its acquisition of backup provider Dropsuite). NinjaOne provides a cloud-native platform for remote monitoring and management (RMM), patch management, IT asset management, software deployment, remote access and control, endpoint backup, and endpoint security, aimed primarily at managed service providers (MSPs) and internal IT departments managing distributed fleets of Windows, Mac, and Linux devices, plus mobile device management. The platform is known in the industry for a fast, modern interface relative to older RMM tools, automation-first workflows, and an emphasis on customer support quality. NinjaOne has scaled rapidly through venture funding, raising a total of roughly 1.18 billion dollars from investors including Iconiq Capital, Summit Partners, and CapitalG (Alphabet's independent growth fund). A 231.5 million dollar Series C round led by ICONIQ Growth in 2024 was followed by a 500 million dollar Series C extension in 2025 co-led by ICONIQ Growth and CapitalG, pushing valuation to roughly 5 billion dollars, and further extensions pushed the valuation to a reported 12.3 billion dollars by mid-2026. By 2025, NinjaOne reported surpassing 30,000 customers, more than 2,000 employees, and management of over 5 million endpoints, with annualized revenue reported around 310 million dollars. NinjaOne does not publish full pricing tiers publicly, stating that per-device costs vary by product bundle and region to avoid interfering with MSP partner pricing; third-party reporting cites per-endpoint pricing roughly in the range of 1.50 dollars per month at high volume (10,000-plus endpoints) up to 3.75 dollars per month for smaller deployments of 50 or fewer endpoints, billed monthly or annually with custom quotes required from sales. All plans reportedly include free, unlimited support and onboarding with no hidden implementation fees. NinjaOne's target customers are MSPs managing client IT environments and internal IT teams at mid-market and enterprise organizations needing centralized endpoint visibility and automation. Its primary competitors include ConnectWise Automate, Datto RMM (Kaseya), Atera, N-able, and Syncro, with NinjaOne differentiating on ease of use, fast deployment, and a modern unified platform that has expanded beyond RMM into backup and security through acquisitions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NinjaOne is a unified IT and endpoint management platform that grew out of NinjaRMM, founded in 2013 by Sal Sferlazza and Chris Matarese and publicly launched in 2015. Headquartered in Austin, Texas, the company has expanded well beyond its original remote monitoring and management (RMM) roots into a broader platform spanning patch management, backup, and endpoint security.

NinjaOne primarily serves managed service providers (MSPs) that manage IT for multiple client organizations, as well as internal IT teams at mid-market and enterprise companies. The platform is widely cited for its modern interface and ease of use compared to legacy RMM tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include remote monitoring and management of Windows, Mac, and Linux endpoints, automated patch management, IT asset inventory tracking, software deployment, and remote access and control for troubleshooting devices from anywhere. The platform also includes mobile device management for managing smartphones and tablets alongside traditional endpoints.

Through acquisitions and internal development, NinjaOne has added endpoint backup and disaster recovery, endpoint security and antivirus management, and IT documentation tools, positioning itself as a single console for most day-to-day IT operations rather than a point solution for any one task.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: NinjaOne does not publish detailed public pricing, using a custom, per-endpoint quote model instead. Reported per-device pricing ranges from roughly 1.50 dollars per month at very high endpoint volumes down to 3.75 dollars per month for small deployments, though exact rates vary by product bundle, region, and whether the customer is an MSP reselling to end clients.

The company states that it withholds published rate cards partly to avoid interfering with how MSP partners price services to their own clients. All plans reportedly include free onboarding, training, and unlimited support with no hidden implementation fees.

features.title: Remote monitoring and management (RMM)
features.description: Continuously monitors the health and status of endpoints across Windows, Mac, and Linux devices, alerting IT teams to issues in real time.

features.title: Automated patch management
features.description: Automates operating system and third-party software patching across managed devices to reduce security exposure and manual effort.

features.title: Remote access and control
features.description: Lets technicians remotely access and control endpoints for troubleshooting without needing physical access to the device.

features.title: IT asset management
features.description: Maintains an inventory of hardware and software assets across managed environments for tracking and compliance.

features.title: Endpoint backup
features.description: Provides backup and disaster recovery for endpoints and, through its Dropsuite acquisition, for cloud data such as Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace.

features.title: Endpoint security
features.description: Integrates antivirus, endpoint detection and response, and security posture management into the same console as RMM.

features.title: Software deployment
features.description: Automates the deployment and updating of software across fleets of managed devices.

features.title: Mobile device management
features.description: Extends management and security policies to mobile devices alongside traditional desktop and server endpoints.

pros: Modern, fast interface widely praised relative to legacy RMM competitors
Free, unlimited customer support and onboarding included
Broad platform covering RMM, patch management, backup, and security in one console
Well-funded with strong growth, reducing risk of the vendor being acquired and discontinued
Strong reputation among MSPs for reliability and ease of deployment
cons: No published, transparent pricing, requiring a sales call for even basic quotes
Per-endpoint pricing model can become expensive at very large scale without negotiated discounts
Primarily aimed at MSPs and IT teams, with less relevance for non-technical business users
Feature depth in specific areas like security can lag dedicated point solutions
Rapid acquisition-driven growth means some newer modules (like backup and security) are less mature than the core RMM product

faqs.question: What is NinjaOne used for?
faqs.answer: NinjaOne is used by managed service providers and internal IT teams to remotely monitor, manage, patch, secure, and back up computers, servers, and mobile devices from a single cloud console.

faqs.question: How much does NinjaOne cost?
faqs.answer: NinjaOne does not publish fixed pricing; costs are quoted per endpoint and vary by product bundle and volume, with third-party reports citing a range of roughly 1.50 to 3.75 dollars per endpoint per month.

faqs.question: Who founded NinjaOne?
faqs.answer: NinjaOne was founded in 2013 as NinjaRMM by Sal Sferlazza and Chris Matarese, with its platform launching publicly in 2015.

faqs.question: Is NinjaOne only for MSPs?
faqs.answer: No, while NinjaOne is heavily used by managed service providers, it is also used by internal IT departments at mid-market and enterprise organizations.

faqs.question: Does NinjaOne offer endpoint security?
faqs.answer: Yes, NinjaOne includes endpoint security and antivirus management alongside its core RMM and patch management features.

faqs.question: How much funding has NinjaOne raised?
faqs.answer: NinjaOne has raised approximately 1.18 billion dollars in total funding, including a 2024 Series C round and 2025 extensions that pushed its valuation to roughly 5 billion dollars, with a reported 12.3 billion dollar valuation by mid-2026.

faqs.question: Does NinjaOne support Mac and Linux, or just Windows?
faqs.answer: NinjaOne supports Windows, Mac, and Linux endpoints, along with mobile device management for smartphones and tablets.

use_cases.title: MSP client fleet management
use_cases.description: Managed service providers use NinjaOne to monitor, patch, and support IT environments for dozens or hundreds of client organizations from a single console.

use_cases.title: Internal IT operations at mid-market companies
use_cases.description: Internal IT departments use NinjaOne to manage patching, remote support, and asset tracking for employee devices across distributed offices.
use_cases.audience: Internal IT teams at mid-market and enterprise organizations

use_cases.title: Unified backup and endpoint security
use_cases.description: Organizations consolidate backup, endpoint security, and RMM into NinjaOne's single platform to reduce the number of vendors and consoles IT staff must manage.
use_cases.audience: IT leaders consolidating their tool stack

features: Optional add-ons for backup and security

enrichment.short_description: Ninox is a low-code database and business app builder that lets teams design custom applications, workflows, and dashboards without writing code, with native macOS, Windows, iOS, and web apps.
enrichment.seo_title: Ninox Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ninox review 2026: explore this low-code database and business app builder's features, pricing plans, pros and cons, and top no-code alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ninox is a low-code database and business application platform founded in 2013 by Frank Bohmer and headquartered in Berlin, Germany. The product lets non-developers and small IT teams build custom business applications -- from CRM systems to inventory trackers to project management tools -- using a visual database builder, a formula-based scripting language, and drag-and-drop form and layout design, without writing traditional code. Ninox differentiates itself from spreadsheet-style no-code tools like Airtable through its relational database engine and native desktop and mobile applications for macOS, Windows, iOS, and the web, alongside offline support that syncs when connectivity returns, which makes it popular with field-based teams and businesses in Europe. The company reports more than 5,000 customers across over 100 partner companies and more than 250,000 users across 6,000-plus companies globally, with an employee count reported in the range of roughly 90 to 150 people (sources vary between about 87 and 92 on formal headcount trackers, with broader LinkedIn ranges up to 200). Ninox has increasingly marketed itself as an 'AI-native' low-code platform, adding AI-assisted app generation and automation features on top of its core database and workflow engine. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free plan that never expires and requires no credit card, offering basic database and app-building functionality for individuals; a Starter plan around 11 euros per user per month; a Professional plan around 22 euros per user per month with more automation, integrations, and admin controls; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier for large deployments, all billed annually plus VAT (with per-user pricing around 25 euros for prior public plan naming, reflecting periodic plan restructuring). Templates are available for common use cases like CRM, project management, and inventory management to accelerate setup. Ninox's target audience is small and mid-sized businesses, agencies, and departmental teams -- particularly in Europe -- that need custom internal tools but lack dedicated software development resources. Its primary competitors include Airtable, Knack, Zoho Creator, Caspio, and Microsoft Power Apps, with Ninox's key differentiators being its relational database model, native offline-capable desktop and mobile apps, and strong presence in the German and broader European market.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ninox is a low-code platform for building custom business applications and databases without writing traditional code. Founded in 2013 by Frank Bohmer and headquartered in Berlin, Germany, the platform is aimed at small and mid-sized businesses that need tailored internal tools -- CRMs, inventory systems, project trackers -- but do not have dedicated software engineering resources.

Unlike spreadsheet-first no-code tools, Ninox is built around a genuine relational database engine paired with native applications for macOS, Windows, iOS, and the web, including offline support that syncs automatically once a device reconnects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users design custom data models visually, then build forms, layouts, and dashboards through drag-and-drop tools, with a formula-based scripting language available for more advanced logic and automation. Pre-built templates for common use cases like CRM, project management, and inventory tracking help teams get started quickly.

Ninox has invested in AI-assisted app generation, letting users describe what they want to build and get a starting application structure, and it supports integrations and API access on higher tiers for connecting to other business systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ninox offers a free plan that never expires and requires no credit card, suitable for individuals or very small teams testing the platform. Paid plans start with a Starter tier around 11 euros per user per month and step up to a Professional tier around 22 euros per user per month with more automation and admin controls.
Enterprise pricing is custom for larger organizations needing advanced security, support, and deployment options. All paid plans are billed annually plus applicable VAT.

features.title: Visual database builder
features.description: Lets users design relational data models and tables through a visual interface without writing SQL or backend code.

features.title: Custom forms and layouts
features.description: Drag-and-drop tools for building custom forms, views, and dashboards tailored to specific business workflows.

features.title: Formula-based scripting
features.description: A built-in scripting language allows advanced automation and calculated fields beyond what drag-and-drop alone can achieve.

features.title: Native desktop and mobile apps
features.description: Dedicated applications for macOS, Windows, and iOS, in addition to a web app, with offline access that syncs when reconnected.

features.title: Prebuilt templates
features.description: Ready-made templates for common use cases such as CRM, inventory management, and project tracking speed up initial setup.

features.title: AI-assisted app building
features.description: AI tools help generate initial app structures and automations from natural-language descriptions of what a user wants to build.

features.title: Team collaboration and permissions
features.description: Role-based access controls let teams share databases and apps while restricting sensitive data to specific users or groups.

features.title: API and integrations
features.description: Higher-tier plans include API access and third-party integrations for connecting Ninox apps to other business systems.

pros: True relational database engine, more powerful than spreadsheet-style no-code tools for complex data
Native offline-capable apps for macOS, Windows, and iOS, not just a web app
Free plan that never expires, good for testing before committing to a paid tier
Strong library of templates for common business use cases
Popular and well-supported in European markets with local-language support
cons: Steeper learning curve than pure drag-and-drop tools due to its formula/scripting layer
Smaller ecosystem of third-party integrations compared to Airtable or Power Apps
Per-user pricing can add up quickly for larger teams
Interface and design conventions can feel dated compared to newer no-code competitors
Advanced automation and admin controls are gated behind higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: What is Ninox used for?
faqs.answer: Ninox is used to build custom business applications and databases, such as CRMs, inventory systems, and project management tools, without writing traditional code.

faqs.question: How much does Ninox cost?
faqs.answer: Ninox offers a free plan that never expires, with paid plans starting around 11 euros per user per month for Starter and 22 euros per user per month for Professional; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Who founded Ninox?
faqs.answer: Ninox was founded in 2013 by Frank Bohmer and is headquartered in Berlin, Germany.

faqs.question: Does Ninox work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ninox's native macOS, Windows, and iOS apps support offline use, syncing data automatically once the device reconnects to the internet.

faqs.question: Is Ninox a no-code or low-code tool?
faqs.answer: Ninox is generally described as low-code: most app-building happens through visual, drag-and-drop tools, but a formula-based scripting language is available for advanced logic.

faqs.question: How does Ninox compare to Airtable?
faqs.answer: Ninox uses a true relational database engine and offers native offline desktop and mobile apps, while Airtable is more spreadsheet-first with a larger integration ecosystem; the choice often depends on data complexity versus integration needs.

faqs.question: Does Ninox offer templates?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ninox provides prebuilt templates for common use cases including CRM, inventory management, and project tracking to help teams get started quickly.

use_cases.title: Custom CRM for small businesses
use_cases.description: Small businesses use Ninox to build a tailored CRM that matches their exact sales process, rather than adapting to a rigid off-the-shelf CRM.

use_cases.title: Field team data collection
use_cases.description: Teams working in the field, such as inspectors or service technicians, use Ninox's offline-capable mobile apps to collect and sync data without needing constant connectivity.

use_cases.title: Departmental workflow apps
use_cases.description: Individual departments within larger organizations use Ninox to build lightweight internal tools for tracking inventory, projects, or operations without involving central IT.

enrichment.short_description: Nitro PDF Pro is a Windows desktop PDF editor from Nitro Software offering PDF creation, editing, conversion, OCR, and e-signature tools with a one-time purchase licensing option.
enrichment.seo_title: Nitro PDF Pro Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nitro PDF Pro review 2026: see how this Windows PDF editor compares on features, one-time and subscription pricing, pros and cons, and Adobe alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nitro PDF Pro is a Windows desktop PDF editing application made by Nitro Software, a company founded in Melbourne, Australia in 2005 to build an alternative to Adobe Acrobat. Over its history, Nitro grew into a broader company offering both desktop software (Nitro Pro) and cloud-based subscription products (Nitro PDF Standard and Plus) covering PDF creation, editing, conversion, combining, OCR (optical character recognition), form filling and signing, and electronic signature workflows. The company went public on the Australian Securities Exchange and, after a period as a listed company, was the subject of competing acquisition interest in 2022 to 2023: Alludo (formerly Corel) made a non-binding proposal to acquire Nitro at 2.00 dollars per share, but Potentia Capital, together with HarbourVest Partners, ultimately completed the acquisition of the remaining public stake in Nitro Software Limited in March 2023 at a final price of 2.20 dollars per share, taking the company private. Nitro PDF Pro specifically refers to the perpetual-license desktop product line (marketed at times as Nitro Pro or Nitro PDF Classic), distinct from Nitro's subscription-based Standard and Plus plans. Nitro PDF Pro is Windows-exclusive, uses a Microsoft Office-style ribbon interface familiar to Office users, and covers core PDF tools -- editing, converting, combining files, filling and signing forms, and OCR for scanned documents -- through either a one-time purchase (reported around 250 dollars per license) or a three-year license term, without the AI features, team collaboration tools, or advanced analytics included in Nitro's cloud subscription tiers. By contrast, Nitro's subscription lineup starts around 15 dollars per month for Nitro PDF Standard, which adds AI productivity features, integrations, and centralized team management via the Nitro Admin Portal, and extends to a custom-quoted Plus plan with enterprise security, advanced analytics, and a dedicated customer success manager; those subscription plans also support Mac and iOS in addition to Windows. Nitro's target customers span individual professionals and small teams who prefer a one-time software purchase (Nitro PDF Pro) up to larger organizations wanting centrally managed, cross-platform PDF and e-signature tools via subscription. Its primary competitors include Adobe Acrobat, Foxit PDF Editor, PDFelement, and Smallpdf, with Nitro differentiating itself through competitive one-time-purchase pricing for its desktop product and a business model that has historically undercut Adobe on cost while offering comparable core PDF editing functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nitro PDF Pro is a Windows desktop PDF editing application built by Nitro Software, a company founded in Melbourne, Australia in 2005 specifically to offer a lower-cost alternative to Adobe Acrobat. The product covers the core tasks most PDF users need: creating, editing, converting, and combining PDF files, filling and signing forms, and running OCR on scanned documents.

Nitro PDF Pro is distinct from Nitro's newer cloud-based subscription products (Nitro PDF Standard and Plus). Where the subscription plans run across Windows, Mac, and iOS with AI features and team management built in, Nitro PDF Pro is a Windows-only desktop application, available as a one-time purchase or multi-year license rather than an ongoing subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The application uses a Microsoft Office-style ribbon interface, making it familiar to users coming from Word or Excel. Core capabilities include full PDF editing (text, images, and pages), converting PDFs to and from formats like Word and Excel, combining multiple files into a single PDF, and filling and digitally signing forms.

Built-in OCR allows users to make scanned, image-based documents searchable and editable. As a perpetual-license product, Nitro PDF Pro does not include the AI productivity tools, advanced analytics, or dedicated customer success support reserved for Nitro's subscription tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nitro PDF Pro is available as a one-time purchase, reported at roughly 250 dollars per license, or as a multi-year license option, appealing to buyers who prefer to avoid ongoing subscription costs.

For comparison, Nitro's subscription lineup starts at around 15 dollars per month for the Nitro PDF Standard plan, which adds AI features and cross-platform support for Windows, Mac, and iOS, while the top-tier Plus plan is custom-quoted for larger organizations with enterprise security and support needs.

features.title: PDF creation and editing
features.description: Create new PDFs or edit existing ones, including text, images, and page-level changes, within a Microsoft Office-style interface.

features.title: File conversion
features.description: Convert PDFs to and from formats such as Word, Excel, and PowerPoint while preserving formatting.

features.title: Combine and split files
features.description: Merge multiple documents into a single PDF or split a large PDF into separate files.

features.title: Form filling and e-signature
features.description: Fill out PDF forms and apply legally binding electronic signatures directly within the application.

features.title: OCR (optical character recognition)
features.description: Converts scanned, image-based documents into searchable and editable text.

features.title: One-time purchase licensing
features.description: Available as a perpetual license or multi-year term rather than requiring an ongoing monthly subscription.

features.title: Batch processing
features.description: Applies actions like conversion or watermarking across multiple PDF files at once to save time on repetitive tasks.

features.title: Windows-native performance
features.description: Built specifically for Windows desktops, integrating with familiar Office-style menus and shortcuts.

pros: One-time purchase option avoids ongoing subscription costs common with Adobe Acrobat
Familiar Microsoft Office-style ribbon interface reduces the learning curve
Includes OCR and e-signature tools without needing separate add-on purchases
Generally priced lower than comparable Adobe Acrobat plans
Strong core PDF editing and conversion accuracy
cons: Windows-only; no native Mac or Linux version of the Pro desktop product
Lacks the AI features, analytics, and team management tools available in Nitro's subscription plans
One-time license may be limited to a fixed license term (reported around three years) rather than indefinite use
Support options are more limited than on paid subscription tiers with dedicated success managers
Interface conventions can feel dated compared to newer, browser-based PDF tools

faqs.question: What is Nitro PDF Pro?
faqs.answer: Nitro PDF Pro is a Windows desktop PDF editing application from Nitro Software that offers PDF creation, editing, conversion, OCR, and e-signature tools through a one-time purchase or multi-year license.

faqs.question: How much does Nitro PDF Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Nitro PDF Pro has been offered around 250 dollars as a one-time license, distinct from Nitro's subscription plans which start around 15 dollars per month.

faqs.question: Is Nitro PDF Pro available on Mac?
faqs.answer: No, Nitro PDF Pro is Windows-only. Mac and iOS support is available through Nitro's subscription-based PDF Standard and Plus plans instead.

faqs.question: Who owns Nitro Software?
faqs.answer: Nitro Software was founded in Melbourne, Australia in 2005 and, after being publicly listed on the Australian Securities Exchange, was taken private in March 2023 when Potentia Capital and HarbourVest Partners completed acquisition of the remaining public shares.

faqs.question: Does Nitro PDF Pro include OCR?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nitro PDF Pro includes optical character recognition (OCR) for converting scanned documents into searchable, editable text.

faqs.question: How does Nitro PDF Pro compare to Adobe Acrobat?
faqs.answer: Nitro PDF Pro offers comparable core PDF editing, conversion, and e-signature functionality to Adobe Acrobat, typically at a lower price point, though Adobe maintains a broader ecosystem and more advanced enterprise features.

faqs.question: Does Nitro PDF Pro include electronic signatures?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nitro PDF Pro includes built-in tools for filling and applying legally binding electronic signatures to PDF forms and documents.

use_cases.title: Individual professional PDF editing
use_cases.description: Professionals who occasionally need to edit, convert, or sign PDFs use Nitro PDF Pro's one-time purchase to avoid recurring subscription fees.

use_cases.title: Small business document workflows
use_cases.description: Small businesses use Nitro PDF Pro on Windows desktops to handle contracts, forms, and scanned paperwork without paying for a full enterprise subscription.

use_cases.title: Digitizing scanned paperwork
use_cases.description: Organizations with large volumes of scanned or paper documents use Nitro PDF Pro's OCR to convert them into searchable, editable digital files.

enrichment.short_description: NocoDB is an open-source, self-hostable Airtable alternative that turns any database into a smart spreadsheet interface, letting teams build no-code apps on top of production data.
enrichment.seo_title: NocoDB Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: NocoDB review 2026: explore this open-source Airtable alternative's features, free and paid pricing tiers, pros and cons, and top no-code alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: NocoDB is an open-source, self-hostable no-code database platform founded by UK-based founder and CEO Naveen Rudrappa, with the open-source project first released around 2019 and the company formally established in the years following. NocoDB positions itself as a free and self-hostable alternative to Airtable, distinguished by its ability to turn any existing SQL database -- including production databases like MySQL, Postgres, and SQL Server -- into a smart spreadsheet-style interface, rather than requiring data to live in a proprietary datastore. This was highlighted in NocoDB's October 2022 TechCrunch coverage, which noted its approach of connecting directly to production data rather than forcing a migration into a separate no-code silo. The company has raised approximately 10.5 million dollars in seed funding from institutional investors including Decibel, OSS Capital, Uncorrelated Ventures, and Together Fund, alongside a notable roster of angel investors including YouTube co-founder Chad Hurley, WordPress creator Matt Mullenweg, Red Hat co-founder Bob Young, and early Google investor Ram Shriram. The open-source core project has been widely adopted, reporting more than 7 million Docker downloads within its first year and over 50,000 GitHub stars in more recent reporting, reflecting strong developer and self-hoster adoption. The product offers six view types (grid, gallery, kanban, calendar, form, and gantt-style views), automation and scripting, dashboards, role-based permissions, and API access for building lightweight internal tools and workflows on top of existing data. NocoDB offers both a free, self-hosted Community edition (fair-code licensed, with unlimited records, storage, and seats) and paid cloud and self-hosted tiers: Cloud Free (1,000 records, 1 GB storage), Cloud Plus at 12 dollars per user per month annually (50,000 records, 20 GB storage), Cloud Business at 24 dollars per user per month annually (300,000 records, SSO, private bases), Cloud Scale at 45 dollars per user per month annually (1,000,000 records, row-level security, audit logs), and a custom-priced Enterprise tier with SCIM and air-gapped deployment options; self-hosted Business and Scale tiers mirror similar pricing per editor. NocoDB is a lean company, reported at around nine employees as of 2026, reflecting an open-source-led, community-driven growth strategy rather than a large go-to-market team. Its target audience spans individual developers and self-hosters who want full data control, alongside startups and enterprises building internal tools on existing databases. Its primary competitors include Airtable, Baserow, NocoBase, Budibase, and Retool, with NocoDB's key differentiator being its ability to connect directly to existing production SQL databases and its fair-code open-source licensing model that allows free self-hosting at unlimited scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NocoDB is an open-source, self-hostable no-code database platform often described as a free alternative to Airtable. Founded by UK-based CEO Naveen Rudrappa with the open-source project emerging around 2019, NocoDB's core differentiator is that it can connect directly to existing SQL databases -- including production databases like MySQL and Postgres -- and turn them into a smart, spreadsheet-style interface, rather than requiring data to be migrated into a proprietary format.

This approach was highlighted in TechCrunch's October 2022 coverage of the company, which framed NocoDB as taking on Airtable by working with production data directly. The project has become one of the more widely adopted open-source no-code tools, with millions of Docker downloads and tens of thousands of GitHub stars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: NocoDB offers multiple ways to view the same underlying data, including grid, gallery, kanban, calendar, and form views, so different team members can interact with data in the format that suits their workflow. Automations, scripting, and dashboards let teams build lightweight internal tools and workflows without a dedicated engineering project.

Because it is fair-code licensed and open source, the Community edition can be self-hosted for free with unlimited records, storage, and seats, appealing strongly to developers and organizations that want full control over their data and infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: NocoDB's self-hosted Community edition is free forever with unlimited records, storage, and seats. Paid cloud plans start with a free tier (1,000 records, 1 GB storage) and step up to Plus at 12 dollars per user per month, Business at 24 dollars per user per month, and Scale at 45 dollars per user per month, all billed annually.

Self-hosted Business and Scale tiers offer similar feature sets to their cloud counterparts, priced per editor rather than per total seat, with a custom-quoted Enterprise tier available for organizations needing SCIM, air-gapped deployment, and dedicated support.

features.title: Connects to existing SQL databases
features.description: Turns existing MySQL, Postgres, or SQL Server databases into a spreadsheet-style interface without requiring data migration.

features.title: Multiple view types
features.description: Supports grid, gallery, kanban, calendar, form, and gantt-style views of the same underlying data for different workflows.

features.title: Open-source and self-hostable
features.description: The Community edition is fair-code licensed and free to self-host with unlimited records, storage, and seats.

features.title: Automations and scripting
features.description: Lets teams build workflow automations and custom scripts to act on data changes without writing a full application.

features.title: Role-based permissions
features.description: Controls access to bases and records by role, supporting private bases and granular team permissions on paid tiers.

features.title: API access
features.description: Exposes REST and other APIs for integrating NocoDB data with external applications and services.

features.title: Dashboards
features.description: Builds visual dashboards on top of underlying data for reporting and at-a-glance insights.

features.title: Row-level security and audit logs
features.description: Higher-tier plans add row-level security and audit logging for compliance-sensitive deployments.

pros: Free, self-hostable Community edition with unlimited records, storage, and seats
Connects directly to existing production databases instead of requiring data migration
Strong open-source adoption with millions of downloads and tens of thousands of GitHub stars
Backed by well-known angel investors from the open-source and tech community
Transparent, published pricing across both cloud and self-hosted tiers
cons: Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance compared to a fully managed SaaS
Smaller company and support team than Airtable, which may mean slower response times
Some advanced features like SSO and row-level security are gated behind paid tiers
Cloud free tier's 1,000-record limit is restrictive for anything beyond small tests
Interface and polish can lag behind more mature, heavily-funded no-code competitors

faqs.question: What is NocoDB used for?
faqs.answer: NocoDB is used to turn existing SQL databases or new data sets into a smart spreadsheet-style interface, letting teams build no-code apps, views, and workflows on top of their data.

faqs.question: Is NocoDB really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, NocoDB's Community edition is open source and fair-code licensed, allowing free self-hosting with unlimited records, storage, and seats. Paid cloud and self-hosted plans add features like SSO, audit logs, and higher usage limits.

faqs.question: How is NocoDB different from Airtable?
faqs.answer: NocoDB is open source and can connect directly to existing production SQL databases, while Airtable is a closed, proprietary platform that requires data to live within its own system.

faqs.question: Who founded NocoDB?
faqs.answer: NocoDB was founded by UK-based founder and CEO Naveen Rudrappa, with the open-source project first released around 2019.

faqs.question: Can NocoDB be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, NocoDB can be self-hosted for free using its open-source Community edition, or organizations can use NocoDB's managed cloud offering instead.

faqs.question: How much does NocoDB's cloud plan cost?
faqs.answer: NocoDB's cloud pricing starts with a free tier, then Plus at 12 dollars per user per month, Business at 24 dollars per user per month, and Scale at 45 dollars per user per month, all billed annually.

faqs.question: Has NocoDB raised funding?
faqs.answer: Yes, NocoDB has raised approximately 10.5 million dollars in seed funding from investors including Decibel, OSS Capital, Uncorrelated Ventures, and Together Fund, along with several notable angel investors.

use_cases.title: Building internal tools on production data
use_cases.description: Engineering and operations teams use NocoDB to build spreadsheet-style internal tools directly on top of existing production databases without duplicating data.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted data control for privacy-sensitive teams
use_cases.description: Organizations with strict data residency or privacy requirements self-host NocoDB's open-source Community edition to keep full control of their infrastructure.

use_cases.title: No-code app building for small teams
use_cases.description: Small teams and startups use NocoDB's views, automations, and dashboards to build lightweight apps and workflows without hiring developers.
